/NTgCalls/Build Guide.xml.title: Introduction
/NTgCalls/Build Guide.xml.description: Being a modern library, requires CMake 3.31+ to be built on your system, on Linux is required at least CLang 20+, libasound2 and libpulse-dev. We recommend using the latest versions of both CMake and CLang. Below, we will guide you through the step-by-step process of installing the prerequisites required to compile NTgCalls on your operating system. In this section, you'll learn how to clone the NTgCalls repository and build the library from source. To begin building NTgCalls from source, you need to clone the GitHub repository. Follow these steps to clone the repo: Now that you have successfully installed all the required prerequisites for NTgCalls, it's time to build the library. Follow these steps to compile NTgCalls on your system. For developers looking to use NTgCalls with C and C++, we provide C Bindings. /PyTgCalls/Basic Methods/Idle.xml.title: Idle
/PyTgCalls/Basic Methods/Idle.xml.description: This function will run indefinitely in order to block the main script execution and prevent it from exiting while having client(s) that are still running in the background. PyTgCalls works by keeping your handlers in a pool of worker threads, which run concurrently outside the main thread. Calling idle() will ensure that the client(s) remain active, by preventing the main script from ending until you decide to quit. Once a signal is received (e.g.: from CTRL+C) the function will terminate and your main script will continue.

example.code:
from pytgcalls import PyTgCalls
from pytgcalls import idle
...

app1 = PyTgCalls(client1)
app2 = PyTgCalls(client2)
app3 = PyTgCalls(client3)

...  # Set handlers up

app1.start()
app2.start()
app3.start()

idle()

items.description: - Stream Change Control: Added keep_open parameter to AudioDescription and VideoDescription, allowing you to force stream changes or let the StreamManager automatically detect them. - JSON Performance Boost: Replaced nlohmann JSON with Boost.JSON for improved parsing and serialization performance. - Android Builds Now Stable: Addressed the experimental-relative-c++-abi-vtables issue on x86_64, making Android builds fully stable and production-ready. - Android Audio Improvements: Now using Oboe for audio handling on Android, providing lower latency and better performance. - Linux Performance Upgrades: Updated to the latest versions of libX11, FFmpeg, mesa, glib, boost and libwebrtc for improved stability and performance. - Bug Fixes: Addressed various bugs for a more stable experience.

- Support for Large Group Calls: PyTgCalls now supports group calls with 20 or more participants in channels, allowing the deprecation of the UnMuteNeeded exception. - RTMP Stream Integration: You can now connect to RTMP streams and record them using the record() method. This enables streaming external RTMP content directly into your group calls. - Group Call Discarding Option: The close parameter has been added to the leave_call() method, allowing you to fully terminate a group call when it's no longer needed. - Enhanced Participant Caching: The get_participants() method now supports proper caching for clients using the no_update parameter, ensuring accurate participant lists without triggering unnecessary updates. - New ChatUpdate Event: The ChatUpdate event has been introduced to track changes such as busy call status. You can now use the filters.chat_update(ChatUpdate.Status.BUSY_CALL) filter to monitor these updates. - New Group Call Participant Event: The UpdatedGroupCallParticipant event enables handling of actions like participants joining from another device or being kicked. Use filters.call_participant(GroupCallParticipant.Action.KICKED) to listen for such events. - Improved Audio/Video Encoding Performance: Audio and video encoding performance has been significantly improved thanks to an optimized FFmpeg build. The latest version takes advantage of advanced CPU instruction sets like AVX2, resulting in faster processing and lower latency. - Bug Fixes: Addressed various bugs for a more stable experience. - Now the pytgcalls.GroupCallParticipant.Action has been moved from pytgcalls.GroupCallParticipant to pytgcalls.UpdatedGroupCallParticipant. 
@call_py.on_update(filters.call_participant())
  async def participant_handler(_: PyTgCalls, update: UpdatedGroupCallParticipant):
-     print(update.participant.user_id, update.participant.action)
+     print(update.participant.user_id, update.action)
The repository AshokShau/TgMusicBot has won the #PyTgCon2K24 contest! It is now officially featured in the Quickstart section of the NTgCalls documentation. Check it out to see an excellent example of what you can build with PyTgCalls!

- Group Calls V2 – Stream Video & Screen Simultaneously: Now you can stream both your screen and video at the same time! Learn more in pytgcalls.types.raw.Stream. - Record Your Streams: PyTgCalls now supports stream recording! Find out how to use it here. - Performance & Stability Boost: We've upgraded WebRTC to the latest version (m132) and optimized the codebase for better stability and speed. - PyTgCalls Now Supports Java for Android: You can now use PyTgCalls in Android apps with Java! Check out Telegram-X for usage details or find the library here. - Massive CPU Optimization: CPU usage reduced by up to 60% and system calls decreased by 90%, making everything more efficient. - Clearer C Binding Error Messages: Debugging is now easier with detailed error messages for C bindings. - Native Media Device Streaming: Stream your microphone, camera, or screen directly with Media Devices. Learn more here. - Better Performance with OpenH264: Now supporting OpenH264 for lower CPU usage and improved performance. - Now the MediaDevices has been refactored, so was renamed the get_screen_devices() and get_audio_devices() to screen_devices() and microphone_devices().

- Private Calls are Now Stable: Easily make private calls, even for groups, with a single method—just use play()! Check out the example here: Private Calls. - Performance Boost: We're now running the latest WebRTC version (m128), bringing key performance fixes. Plus, with the latest pybind11 update, issues with Python's garbage collection have been resolved for a smoother experience. - New "adjust_by_height" Feature: Adjust video resolution by height instead of width, giving you more flexibility in how you manage your video streams. Learn more here. - New Event Handlers (remove_handler & add_handler): These methods allow you to register listeners for existing events just like the decorators in the library. Use them to handle events such as "stream ended" or "new user connected" within PyTgCalls. Find out more here. - Improved Documentation: Enjoy clearer, more detailed documentation with fresh examples, a new UI, a powerful search engine, and step-by-step guidance to help you get started quickly. - Bug Fixes: Addressed various bugs for a more stable experience. Thank You All: We've reached a new milestone with over 400 thousand of active monthly users! We're grateful for your support and feedback, which has helped us improve PyTgCalls and make it the go-to library for all your real-time communication needs.

- Private Calls: Now you can make private calls by just using a single method for also group calls, by using play(). - Code Rework: The code has been reworked to improve performance, stability and to make it more readable. - Async Support: NTgCalls now supports asynchronous methods for improved performance. - Filters for Updates: Added filters for updates to allow for more granular control over updates, More Info. - Improved Documentation: Updated documentation with more examples, new UI and comprehensive guidance. - Bug Fixes: Addressed various bugs for a more stable experience. - Now methods like change_stream() and join_group_call() was removed, now you can use play() for both private, group calls and changing streams.

- Reworked GroupCall type, now is Call and now is more powerful and easy to use. - Removed JoinedGroupCallParticipant and LeftGroupCallParticipant classes, now you can see the action in GroupCallParticipant class. - Removed all legacy types and input stream, now is a mandatory to use MediaStream for all streams. - Now ping() from Client is not anymore a coroutine, now is a normal method. - Removed get_active_call() and get_call(), now is a mandatory to use calls() from Client. - Renamed leave_group_call to leave_call(). - Moved MediaStream flags under MediaStream.Flags instead of MediaStream. - Now invite_hash, join_as and auto_start are part of GroupCallConfig instead of join_group_call. - Renamed additional_ffmpeg_parameters to ffmpeg_parameters in MediaStream. - For more info about breaking changes, check the Examples, if we missed something, please open an issue. We're pleased to announce that now is available an unofficial porting of NTgCalls for Rust, for more info, check the repository.

- Static Typing: PyTgCalls is now fully statically typed, ensuring that the types of each method and class are thoroughly checked. - YtDlp Support: MediaStream now supports links from YouTube using YtDlp, for example: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iSbLHrrqmoM - MediaStream Quality Support: MediaStream now accepts enhanced quality options for audio and video parameters. For audio, use AudioQuality, and for video, use VideoQuality.

We're pleased to announce that pre-compiled wheels optimized for ARM64 devices are now available.

- Bug Fixes: Addressed various bugs for a more stable experience. As we said in the last post, now PyTgCalls 0.8.6 has reached the end of life, so this update is completely incompatible with this or previous versions. We have also deprecated most of the Stream Piped methods, here's the list: - AudioImagePiped - AudioPiped - AudioVideoPiped - CaptureAudioDevice - CaptureAVDesktop - CaptureAVDeviceDesktop - CaptureVideoDesktop - VideoPiped

- NTgCalls Integration: The PyTgCalls core has been updated to use NTgCalls instead of TgCallsJS, making it more portable and eliminating overhead. Now, you only need to install ffmpeg on your machine, resulting in improved performance. - Hydrogram Support: Official support for Hydrogram, a new mtproto client based on Pyrogram, providing new possibilities and enhancing the overall experience. - New Documentation: Revamped documentation with improved clarity, comprehensive examples, and up-to-date guidance for developers. - Bug Fixes: Addressed various bugs for a more stable experience. Thank You All: Celebrating over 100 thousand Downloads! The project has seen remarkable growth, averaging around 8,000 downloads per week since its launch. We appreciate the community's strong interest and support. End-of-Life (EOL) Dates: Due to changes in Pyrogram and significant time since the release of PyTgCalls 0.8.X, different versions are marked for end-of-life: - 0.8.0, 0.8.1, 0.8.2, 0.8.3, 0.8.4, 0.8.5: Support ended due to outdated versions. - 0.8.6: Support will continue until December 18 due to Pyrogram archiving. - 0.9.X: Current Long-Term Support. - 1.0.X: Latest version with high-support rate.

- Time Tracking: Added the ability to determine the time of the currently playing audio/video. For more details, check Played Time. - New Errors: Introduced two new errors, UnMuteNeeded and RTMPStreamNeeded. - End of Life for Python3.6: Support for Python 3.6 has reached the end of life and is no longer maintained. - Security Fix: Addressed a security issue with the ID generation process to enhance overall security. - Multi-Thread Removal: Removed Multi-Thread Py-TgCalls due to no bug fixes by Node.js for over 4 years.

As we know, Google is in charge of financing Python. Due to the recent events with the Node.js situation, PyPI ran for cover, thanks to the strong collaboration between Python and the Google Open Source Security Team they offered 4000 Titan Tokens for free available only in some regions of the world. PyPI to distribute these security tokens was based on a criterion, that is; any project in the top 1% of downloads in the previous 6 months is considered critical, at the moment only 3500 projects are considered out of 350 thousand are considered as such and py-tgcalls is considered a critical project. According to statistics last month, it was downloaded 684 thousand times.

- WebRTC Retry Fixes: Addressed issues with WebRTC retry for more reliable connections. - Asynchronous Method Invocation: All methods, such as join_group_call, now use asyncio future, waiting for responses from Node.js asynchronously.

- Internal FFmpeg Conversion: Introduced internal FFmpeg conversion for AudioPiped, AudioVideoPiped, and AudioImagePiped for seamless multimedia handling. 
call_py.join_group_call(
    -1001234567890,
    AudioVideoPiped(
        'input.webm',
    ),
    stream_type=StreamType().pulse_stream,
)
- Remote Stream Support: Added support for remote streams for more diverse streaming options. - Stream Image with Audio: Added the ability to stream images with audio using AudioImagePiped. - GetParticipants and OnParticipantChange: New functionalities for handling participants in group calls. - Browser Constants as Headers: Added browser constants as headers for FFmpeg. - Python 3.10 Support: Added compatibility with Python 3.10. - Proportion Video Support: Added support for all proportion video formats for a more versatile video experience. - Communication Fix: Addressed stderr overflow by adding a reader for improved Node.js communication. - WebRTC Connection Retry: Added automatic retry if WebRTC connection fails for more reliable connections. - Lighter Buffer Reader: Optimized the Buffer Reader for lighter resource usage. - Multiple Ping Requests: Now supports multiple ping requests for improved responsiveness.

- Custom Exceptions: Added custom exceptions for more robust error handling. - Platform Support: Added support for Windows, macOS, and Linux Arm64. - PyTgCalls Re-Base: Code cleanup and fixes for a more streamlined and efficient codebase. - Fully Async: The library is now fully asynchronous for improved performance. - Communication Update: Removed the internal socket server for Node.js communication between Python, replaced with stdin and stdout. - CustomAPI Renewed: CustomAPI upgraded to version 2.1 with new features and improvements. - RawUpdate Enhancement: RawUpdate renewed with PyTgCalls Object Update for better handling of updates. - Method Name Change: .run() is now .start() and no longer blocks code execution. - Logging Update: Now logs are directed to Python Logging for improved logging capabilities. - Voice Call Handling: If the stream is deleted, the userbot will exit the voice call by printing an error in RawUpdate. - Bug Fixes: Addressed various bugs for a more stable experience.

- Multi-core support: PyTgCalls now supports multiple cores for enhanced performance. - Remote version check: Added the ability to check for the latest version available on GitHub remotely. - CustomAPI 2.0: Upgraded to CustomAPI version 2.0 with new features and improvements. - Security Fix: Addressed a security vulnerability for a more secure experience. - Function execution without Node.js Core waiting: Users can now call functions without waiting for the Node.js Core.

- Fixed PyPi builds: Fixed an issue where PyTgCalls compiled without the JavaScript core - Improved join_group_call error: The join_group_call error is now more descriptive - Bug Fixes: Addressed various bugs for a more stable experience.

- Bug Fixes: Addressed various bugs for a more stable experience. Deprecation Notice As we said in the last post, now PyTgCalls 0.8.6 has reached the end of life, so this update is completely incompatible with this or previous versions. We have also deprecated most of the Stream Piped methods, here's the list: - AudioImagePiped - AudioPiped - AudioVideoPiped - CaptureAudioDevice - CaptureAVDesktop - CaptureAVDeviceDesktop - CaptureVideoDesktop - VideoPiped

/PyTgCalls/Changelogs/1.2.X.xml.title: Changelog 1.2.X: PyTgCalls v1.2.X
/PyTgCalls/Changelogs/1.2.X.xml.description:
Static Typing, YtDlp support and More What's new? - Static Typing: PyTgCalls is now fully statically typed, ensuring that the types of each method and class are thoroughly checked. - YtDlp Support: MediaStream now supports links from YouTube using YtDlp, for example: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iSbLHrrqmoM - MediaStream Quality Support: MediaStream now accepts enhanced quality options for audio and video parameters. For audio, use AudioQuality, and for video, use VideoQuality.

+         AudioQuality.STUDIO,
-         VideoParameters.from_quality(VideoQuality.FHD_1080p),
+         VideoQuality.FHD_1080p,
      ),
  )
- Bug Fixes: Addressed various bugs for a more stable experience. Breaking Changes - Now all raw methods like AudioParameters, VideoParameters, AudioStream, VideoStream, and Stream are all under pytgcalls.types.raw instead of pytgcalls.types. - The exception class pytgcalls.exceptions.TelegramServerError is now part of ntgcalls.TelegramServerError. Additional Notes We're pleased to announce that pre-compiled wheels optimized for ARM64 devices are now available.

/PyTgCalls/Changelogs/2.0.X.xml.title: Changelog 2.0.X: PyTgCalls v2.0.X
/PyTgCalls/Changelogs/2.0.X.xml.description:
Private Calls, Code Rework, Async Support and more! What's new? - Private Calls: Now you can make private calls by just using a single method for also group calls, by using play(). - Code Rework: The code has been reworked to improve performance, stability and to make it more readable. - Async Support: NTgCalls now supports asynchronous methods for improved performance. - Filters for Updates: Added filters for updates to allow for more granular control over updates, More Info. - Improved Documentation: Updated documentation with more examples, new UI and comprehensive guidance. - Bug Fixes: Addressed various bugs for a more stable experience. Breaking Changes - Now methods like change_stream() and join_group_call() was removed, now you can use play() for both private, group calls and changing streams.

- Reworked GroupCall type, now is Call and now is more powerful and easy to use. - Removed JoinedGroupCallParticipant and LeftGroupCallParticipant classes, now you can see the action in GroupCallParticipant class. - Removed all legacy types and input stream, now is a mandatory to use MediaStream for all streams. - Now ping() from Client is not anymore a coroutine, now is a normal method. - Removed get_active_call() and get_call(), now is a mandatory to use calls() from Client. - Renamed leave_group_call to leave_call(). - Moved MediaStream flags under MediaStream.Flags instead of MediaStream. - Now invite_hash, join_as and auto_start are part of GroupCallConfig instead of join_group_call. - Renamed additional_ffmpeg_parameters to ffmpeg_parameters in MediaStream. - For more info about breaking changes, check the Examples, if we missed something, please open an issue. /PyTgCalls/Changelogs/2.1.X.xml.title: Changelog 2.1.X: PyTgCalls v2.1.X
/PyTgCalls/Changelogs/2.1.X.xml.description:
Enhanced Group Calls, Recording, and More! What's new? - Group Calls V2 – Stream Video & Screen Simultaneously: Now you can stream both your screen and video at the same time! Learn more in pytgcalls.types.raw.Stream. - Record Your Streams: PyTgCalls now supports stream recording! Find out how to use it here. - Performance & Stability Boost: We've upgraded WebRTC to the latest version (m132) and optimized the codebase for better stability and speed. - PyTgCalls Now Supports Java for Android: You can now use PyTgCalls in Android apps with Java! Check out Telegram-X for usage details or find the library here. - Massive CPU Optimization: CPU usage reduced by up to 60% and system calls decreased by 90%, making everything more efficient. - Clearer C Binding Error Messages: Debugging is now easier with detailed error messages for C bindings. - Native Media Device Streaming: Stream your microphone, camera, or screen directly with Media Devices. Learn more here. - Better Performance with OpenH264: Now supporting OpenH264 for lower CPU usage and improved performance. /PyTgCalls/Changelogs/2.2.11.xml.title: Changelog 2.2.11: PyTgCalls v2.2.11
/PyTgCalls/Changelogs/2.2.11.xml.description: Enhanced Android Stability, Performance, and More! What's new? - Stream Change Control: Added keep_open parameter to AudioDescription and VideoDescription, allowing you to force stream changes or let the StreamManager automatically detect them. - JSON Performance Boost: Replaced nlohmann JSON with Boost.JSON for improved parsing and serialization performance. - Android Builds Now Stable: Addressed the experimental-relative-c++-abi-vtables issue on x86_64, making Android builds fully stable and production-ready. - Android Audio Improvements: Now using Oboe for audio handling on Android, providing lower latency and better performance. - Linux Performance Upgrades: Updated to the latest versions of libX11, FFmpeg, mesa, glib, boost and libwebrtc for improved stability and performance. - Bug Fixes: Addressed various bugs for a more stable experience.

/PyTgCalls/Changelogs/2.2.X.xml.title: Changelog 2.2.X: PyTgCalls v2.2.X
/PyTgCalls/Changelogs/2.2.X.xml.description:
Support for Large Group Calls, RTMP Streams, and More! What's new? - Support for Large Group Calls: PyTgCalls now supports group calls with 20 or more participants in channels, allowing the deprecation of the UnMuteNeeded exception. - RTMP Stream Integration: You can now connect to RTMP streams and record them using the record() method. This enables streaming external RTMP content directly into your group calls. - Group Call Discarding Option: The close parameter has been added to the leave_call() method, allowing you to fully terminate a group call when it's no longer needed. - Enhanced Participant Caching: The get_participants() method now supports proper caching for clients using the no_update parameter, ensuring accurate participant lists without triggering unnecessary updates. - New ChatUpdate Event: The ChatUpdate event has been introduced to track changes such as busy call status. You can now use the filters.chat_update(ChatUpdate.Status.BUSY_CALL) filter to monitor these updates. - New Group Call Participant Event: The UpdatedGroupCallParticipant event enables handling of actions like participants joining from another device or being kicked. Use filters.call_participant(GroupCallParticipant.Action.KICKED) to listen for such events. - Improved Audio/Video Encoding Performance: Audio and video encoding performance has been significantly improved thanks to an optimized FFmpeg build. The latest version takes advantage of advanced CPU instruction sets like AVX2, resulting in faster processing and lower latency. - Bug Fixes: Addressed various bugs for a more stable experience. Breaking Changes - Now the pytgcalls.GroupCallParticipant.Action has been moved from pytgcalls.GroupCallParticipant to pytgcalls.UpdatedGroupCallParticipant.

+     print(update.participant.user_id, update.action)

/PyTgCalls/Handling Updates.xml.title: Handling Updates
/PyTgCalls/Handling Updates.xml.description: Calling API methods sequentially is cool, but what if, for example, the list of participants changes? This page covers updates and how to handle such events in PyTgCalls. Let’s have a look at how they work. First of all, let's define what these updates are. As hinted earlier, updates are events that happen in PyTgCalls, such as joining a group call, changes in the participant list, stream ended etc. They are meant to notify you about a new specific state change. These updates are handled by registering one or more callback functions in your app using . Each handler deals with a specific event. When a matching update arrives from Telegram or NTgCalls, the registered callback function will be called back by the framework, and its body will execute. Decorators To explain how handlers work, let's examine the one that will be in charge of handling updates, which occur when a video stream ends. The same setup logic is shared with every type of handler, and you should not have troubles settings them up once you have learned from this section. The most elegant way to register a stream end handler is by using the decorator: on_stream_end() The defined function, which accepts two arguments (client, update), will be executed whenever a new update arrives. The method takes any handler instance that wraps around your defined callback function and registers it in your Client. It is useful in case you want to programmatically add handlers. add_handler()

example.code:
from pytgcalls import PyTgCalls, filters
from pytgcalls.types import Update
from pytgcalls.types import MediaStream
...
app = PyTgCalls(client)

@app.on_update(filters.stream_end())
async def my_handler(client: PyTgCalls, update: Update):
    if isinstance(update, StreamVideoEnded):
        await pytgcalls.play(
            -1001185324811,
            'http://docs.evostream.com/sample_content/assets/sintel1m720p.mp4',
        )

app.run()

The easiest way to install and upgrade PyTgCalls to its latest stable version is by using pip: PyTgCalls is always evolving: although new releases on PyPI are only published when a significant number of changes are added, this doesn’t mean you can’t try new features right now! In case you’d like to try out the latest PyTgCalls’ features, the is constantly updated with new changes; you can install the development version straight from the branch using this command (note “master.zip” in the link): To verify that PyTgCalls is correctly installed, open a Python shell and import it. If no errors show up you are good to go.

/PyTgCalls/Using Handlers.xml.title: Using Handlers
/PyTgCalls/Using Handlers.xml.description: Handlers are used to instruct PyTgCalls to execute a specific function when a certain event occurs. For a much more convenient way to handle updates, you can use the instead. decorators To register a handler, you need to use the method. This method takes two arguments: the first is the callback function, and the second is the filter. To remove a handler, you need to use the method. This method takes the callback function as its only argument.

example.code:
from pytgcalls import PyTgCalls
from pytgcalls.types import Update
from pytgcalls import filters as call_filters
...

client = # Here Your MTProto Client
app = PyTgCalls(client)

async def handler(client: PyTgCalls, update: Update):
    print(update)
...
app.add_handler(handler, call_filters.stream_end())
...
